chunks.principle: Hazards on great courses are placed to challenge the golfer who bites off more than they can chew, not to punish the player who plays within their means.
chunks.application: If a bunker or water hazard seems intimidating, look for the safer route the architect deliberately left open. The penalty is reserved for those who gamble, not for those who play smart.

chunks.principle: Not every hole demands driver. Many holes are designed so that the optimal play off the tee is a club that leaves you in the widest part of the fairway at your comfortable approach distance.
chunks.application: Before pulling driver, look at where the fairway narrows and where the trouble is. A 3-wood or hybrid to the fat part of the fairway often sets up an easier second shot.

chunks.principle: Architects design courses with a 'line of charm' — a route that tempts bold play — and a 'line of instinct' — a safer, more conservative route that still allows a reasonable score.
chunks.application: Identify both routes on every hole. Unless your game is sharp that day, take the line of instinct. The conservative route is always there by design.

chunks.principle: Greens are designed with a preferred angle of approach. The architect places hazards and contours to reward shots from a specific side of the fairway.
chunks.application: Before your tee shot, look at the green and figure out which side gives the best angle in. Your tee shot strategy should be backwards-planned from the ideal approach angle.

chunks.principle: Strategic design means every hole offers multiple paths of varying risk. The greater the risk, the greater the potential reward — but the safe play is always viable.
chunks.application: On risk-reward holes, honestly assess your skill level and current form. The architect designed the safe route to yield bogey at worst; the risky route might yield birdie but also double bogey.

chunks.principle: Great courses are designed so that a player who avoids the hazards and finds the green can always make par or bogey, regardless of skill level.
chunks.application: Your primary goal should be avoiding the big number. If you can keep the ball in play and give yourself a putt for par on most holes, you're playing the course as the architect intended.

chunks.principle: Architects use visual intimidation — deep bunkers, water carries, narrow corridors — to create anxiety. Understanding this is a trick helps you see through it.
chunks.application: When a hole looks terrifying from the tee, remind yourself the architect is playing with your emotions. Measure the actual carry distances and landing areas; they're usually more generous than they appear.

chunks.principle: Par-3 holes are the architect's canvas for strategic challenge. The green complex, not the tee shot distance, is where the real test lies.
chunks.application: On par 3s, club selection should prioritize hitting the correct portion of the green over pin-hunting. Missing to the wrong side of a well-designed par-3 green creates very difficult up-and-downs.

chunks.principle: Par 5s are designed with decision points: the second shot is the critical strategic moment where the player chooses between going for the green and laying up to a preferred distance.
chunks.application: On par 5s, don't automatically try to get as close as possible. Laying up to your favorite wedge distance from a good angle often produces better results than a hero shot from 230 yards.

chunks.principle: Classic courses are designed to play differently depending on wind direction. Holes that are birdie opportunities downwind become survival tests into the wind.
chunks.application: Adjust your strategy entirely when the wind shifts. A hole you drove yesterday with a 7-iron approach might require a layup strategy when the wind is against you.

chunks.principle: Fairway bunkers are typically placed at the distance where a good drive lands. They define the hole's strategic choice, not punish bad shots.
chunks.application: If your drive doesn't reach the fairway bunker, you might actually be playing the safer route. Don't feel you need to carry bunkers that are set at tour-player distances.

chunks.principle: The best strategy is to play away from the worst trouble. Architects create 'bailout' areas — spots where a miss is relatively benign — and these should be your targets when uncertain.
chunks.application: On every approach shot, identify where the biggest trouble is and aim away from it. The bailout side usually offers a simple chip or putt, while the trouble side leads to double bogey.

chunks.principle: Green surrounds are as important as the green surface itself. Architects shape the areas around greens to create varying difficulty depending on where you miss.
chunks.application: When you can't be precise, miss on the side with the flattest ground around the green. A ball that runs off to a mowed area is always better than one in a deep bunker or thick rough.

chunks.principle: Architects design teeing grounds at angles that either invite or discourage certain shot shapes. The tee box alignment itself is a hint about the ideal play.
chunks.application: Notice which direction the tee box points. If it aims slightly left of center, the architect may be encouraging a fade. Let the tee alignment guide your strategy.

chunks.principle: Driveable par 4s are designed to tempt you with a possible eagle or birdie while hiding severe punishment for a miss. The layup almost always yields a better average score for amateurs.
chunks.application: On short par 4s, calculate honestly: if you go for the green, what percentage of the time do you reach it vs. end up in serious trouble? For most amateurs, the layup-and-wedge strategy scores lower.
